children.name: Carcharodon
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the great white shark, the largest predatory fish on Earth and an apex predator of temperate and subtropical coastal oceans, whose torpedo body and rows of serrated teeth make it unmistakable.

children.name: Isurus
children.description: The mako sharks, two species renowned as the fastest sharks in the ocean, with streamlined bodies, crescent-shaped tails, and endothermy that allow sustained high-speed pursuit of tuna and billfish in open oceans.

children.name: Lamna
children.description: The porbeagle and salmon shark, two robust cold-water lamnid sharks with endothermic physiology that enables them to maintain elevated body temperatures and remain active in near-freezing North Atlantic and North Pacific waters.

children.name: Cetorhinus
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the basking shark, the world's second-largest fish, a slow-moving filter feeder that strains zooplankton from the water as it cruises near the surface of temperate and boreal oceans.

children.name: Alopias
children.description: The thresher sharks, three species instantly recognisable by their enormously elongated upper caudal fin lobe — which they crack like a whip to stun schooling fish — found in tropical and temperate oceans globally.

children.name: Carcharias
children.description: The sand tiger shark — a large, slow-moving coastal shark with a distinctive habit of gulping air at the surface to achieve neutral buoyancy, the only shark known to do so. Its mouthful of long, slender, protruding teeth gives it a fearsome appearance wildly disproportionate to its generally placid temperament.

children.name: Carcharias taurus
children.description: Known as the grey nurse shark in Australia and the spotted ragged-tooth shark in South Africa, this large coastal predator is Critically Endangered in the southwestern Pacific. It has one of the lowest reproductive rates of any shark: the two most developed embryos in each uterus consume all remaining eggs and siblings before birth (intrauterine cannibalism, or adelphophagy), meaning females produce at most two pups every two years. This, combined with heavy historical fishing pressure, has made recovery painfully slow. Its air-gulping neutral buoyancy trick — unique among sharks — allows it to hover motionless in the water column, a behaviour that makes it a popular aquarium exhibit.

children.name: Odontaspis
children.description: The deepwater sand tigers — two poorly known lamniform sharks of the open ocean and deep continental slopes, superficially similar to Carcharias but occupying deeper, more pelagic habitats and rarely encountered alive.

children.name: Odontaspis ferox
children.description: A wide-ranging but rarely observed deepwater shark found in tropical and warm-temperate seas worldwide, typically on or near the continental shelf at depths of 10–420 metres. Distinguished from the common sand tiger by its smaller second dorsal fin and fewer teeth rows. Most known from fisheries bycatch and the occasional beach stranding; live sightings by divers are uncommon events. IUCN Vulnerable; its low reproductive rate makes it sensitive to even modest levels of incidental capture.

children.name: Odontaspis noronhai
children.description: One of the least-known large sharks in the world, described in 1955 from a single specimen and known from fewer than thirty confirmed records globally. It inhabits deep oceanic water, possibly migrating vertically between depths of 600–1000 metres and the surface at night. Its most distinctive feature is its enormous eyes — hence the common name — adapted for hunting in near-darkness. Named after the Brazilian Fernando de Noronha archipelago where one of the few specimens was collected. IUCN Vulnerable purely on precautionary grounds; basic aspects of its biology and distribution remain entirely unknown.

children.name: Pseudocarcharias
children.description: The crocodile shark — a monotypic genus containing the smallest living lamniform, a slender oceanic species named for its habit of snapping its jaws violently when caught, much like a crocodile.

children.name: Pseudocarcharias kamoharai
children.description: The smallest member of the order Lamniformes at under 110 cm, the crocodile shark is a highly oceanic species found in tropical open-ocean waters globally, undertaking deep vertical migrations — descending to at least 590 metres by day and ascending toward the surface at night to feed on fish, squid and shrimp. Its needle-sharp teeth, relatively enormous eyes, and highly protrusible jaws are adaptations for capturing agile prey in near-darkness. It is frequently caught as bycatch in pelagic longline fisheries targeting tuna; the meat and liver oil are used locally. IUCN Vulnerable. Named after Masao Kamohara, the Japanese ichthyologist who first described it.

children.name: Mitsukurina
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the goblin shark, a rare deep-sea species whose distinctively elongated flat rostrum and highly protrusible jaws make it one of the most bizarre-looking of all sharks.

children.name: Megachasma
children.description: A monotypic genus containing the megamouth shark, a little-known deep-sea filter feeder discovered only in 1976, with a cavernous luminescent mouth used to attract and engulf small crustaceans and jellyfish.
